I’m Kunal Arora, Co-founder of rego, where we’re building the future of automotive sustainability through SMART Repair technology and circular mobility practices.

At rego, we’re redefining automotive aftercare by building a SMART Repair and Refurbishment ecosystem that focuses on repairing over replacing. Our technology refurbishes alloy wheels, headlights, and exterior plastics — saving costs for insurers, reducing waste, and cutting CO₂ emissions.

Today, rego operates across Delhi NCR, Mumbai, and Bengaluru, refurbishing 500+ components monthly, and aims to cross 1,200+ monthly by July 2026.
I’m deeply passionate about:
• Sustainable automotive ecosystems
• Circular economy in mobility
• Growth strategy & operational excellence
• Building scalable, purpose-driven startups

I have previously worked with PRISM’s(fka OYO) Central Demand Team, working directly under Ritesh Agarwal. During my time there, I witnessed Prism’s explosive growth from 10 to 200 cities and 3 countries, helping scale initiatives like college barter deals, offline demand expansion, and even leading Prism’s first international expansion in Malaysia. Later, I built Prism's food backend and scaled it to $3M ARR.

My next chapter was GoMechanic, where I joined as the 14th employee and took a 50% pay cut to pursue my passion for automotive tech. I founded and scaled the Insurance Claims Vertical from 1 intern to 100 team members, expanded to 350+ workshops across 50+ cities, and grew revenues to $8M ARR. I also spearheaded GoMechanic Accessories’ international expansion to the Middle East.

At rego, our mission is simple yet ambitious: To make “rego over Replace” the new standard for automotive sustainability in India.

martinnjounkwe.li_headline: INSEAD MBA ’26D | ex-World Bank | Exploring Technology, Finance, and Development in African & Middle East markets
martinnjounkwe.li_about: I spent a decade in finance before stopping to think about it properly — five years at the World Bank, and before that Marriott and Morgan Stanley.  In January 2026 I left to do the MBA at INSEAD — one year across Singapore, Paris, and Abu Dhabi. I chose it specifically over the two-year US programs and the Executive route. I didn't want more school at the edges; I wanted a full stop. Space to think. What I'm using the year for: exploring the overlap of technology, finance, and development in African markets. Specifically how capital actually reaches productive activity — through venture capital, private equity, infrastructure finance, development institutions, and sovereign wealth funds — and where the real bottlenecks are. Not the ones in the reports. The ones on the ground. I grew up in Cameroon, did my undergrad in England, and spent over a decade in the US before Singapore. Fluent in English and French, and the markets I'm most interested in tend to speak one or the other. I'm writing publicly about what I'm learning as I go. If you work in any of these worlds, or know someone who'd be open to a conversation, I'd love an intro.

maj-ravipratap.li_headline: INSEAD MBA candidate (‘26D) | Indian Army Veteran | Co-Founder - Guided Ambitions | Product Strategy & Operations
maj-ravipratap.li_about: There's a kind of clarity that only comes when the stakes are real.Serving at 18,000 feet, I learned that most of what we call strategy is really just preparation for the moment when everything stops going as per plan. The first casualty is your plan itself. Doesn't mean, you don't plan. You develop a muscle for decision making.  A decade in the Indian Army gave me that education, not in a classroom, but across 360 - kilometer logistics chains, 190+ face-to-face negotiations with military counterparts at and for one of the world's most contested borders, and programs that had to actually work for 200+ indigenous families in disputed territory, coordinated across military, civil, and government agencies who didn't always agree.What I took from that isn't a set of achievements. It's a way of thinking: the gap between a strategy and an outcome is always execution, and execution is always a people problem.After the Army, I tested that instinct differently. I co-founded a veteran transition platform that placed 80% of its cohort into MBA and consulting roles, then built operations from scratch at a defense startup until we had 50+ military contracts.Now I'm at INSEAD, finishing an MBA and doing something I should have done earlier: building the frameworks to match the judgment I already have.I'm actively exploring roles in consulting, strategy, operations, and product management, globally. If you work in these spaces and are curious about what someone with this background brings to the table, I'd genuinely welcome the conversation.

romanshi-gupta.li_headline: INSEAD MBA | Evaluation & Learning | Strategic Advisory
romanshi-gupta.li_about:
Working at the intersection of people, purpose, planet and profit. Driven to ensure a sustainable and equitable future. Passionate about transforming evidence into action.

My journey started with a conversation I had with a young boy at a government school in 2019 during an undergraduate internship supporting a foundation's educational grant design and deployment. Contrary to the funders' assumptions, his only request was strengthened fencing around the school to prevent vandalism of the natural space. This sparked my passion for community-centred design, co-creating solutions and tackling root causes over 'bandaid' solutions.

Since 2020, I have supported and led comprehensive evaluations in development programmes (deploying approximately £100m+) across 10+ countries. I am skilled at managing complex, multi-stakeholder projects across geographies, delivering actionable insights that have informed policy decisions affecting 1M+ beneficiaries.

I am seeking to transition from impact evaluations to social enterprise leadership, combining my on-ground, field experience with strategic business acumen and managerial skills. My goal is to enable and facilitate sustainable, economically viable solutions for underserved communities, through scaling ventures or improving business operations, 'for Asia, by Asia, within Asia'.

Key Competencies: Qualitative and Mixed-Methods Research/Analysis | Impact Evaluation | Cross-Cultural Leadership | Stakeholder Engagement | Project Management

Languages: English | Hindi | French

Geographies (remote and in-person): South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia (10+ countries)
